chapter: Chapter 3: Electricity: Circuits and Their Components

questions.question: Derive the formula for total resistance in a series circuit and solve a numerical problem with three resistors.
questions.answer: In series: R_total = R1 + R2 + R3. Example: R1=5Ω, R2=10Ω, R3=15Ω. R_total = 5+10+15 = 30Ω.

questions.question: Derive the formula for total resistance in a parallel circuit and solve a numerical example with three resistors.
questions.answer: In parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3. Example: R1=6Ω, R2=3Ω, R3=2Ω. 1/R_total = 1/6+1/3+1/2=1. R_total = 1Ω.

questions.question: Explain with calculations how voltage divides among resistors in series.
questions.answer: Voltage across resistor in series: V = (R/R_total) × V_total. Example: R1=4Ω, R2=6Ω, V_total=10V. V1 = (4/10)×10 = 4V, V2 = (6/10)×10 = 6V.

questions.question: Calculate current drawn by a parallel circuit with a 12 V battery and two bulbs of 6Ω and 12Ω.
questions.answer: Current through 6Ω: I1=V/R=12/6=2A, through 12Ω: I2=12/12=1A. Total current I_total=2+1=3A.

questions.question: Calculate energy consumed by a 60 W bulb connected to 220 V mains for 5 hours.
questions.answer: Energy E = Power × Time = 60 × 5 = 300 Wh = 0.3 kWh.

questions.question: Calculate the current in a series circuit with three resistors of 5Ω, 10Ω, 15Ω connected to a 12 V battery.
questions.answer: R_total = 5+10+15=30Ω. Current I = V/R = 12/30 = 0.4A.
answer_outline: Use Ohm's law to find current

questions.question: Calculate total resistance and current for two resistors 6Ω and 3Ω connected in parallel with a 12 V battery.
questions.answer: 1/R_total = 1/6 + 1/3 = 0.5. R_total = 2Ω. I_total = V/R = 12/2 = 6A.

questions.question: Explain with calculations why adding resistors in series reduces current through the circuit.
questions.answer: I = V/R_total. Series resistors: R_total = R1 + R2 + R3. As R_total increases, I decreases. Example: V=12V, R1=4Ω, R2=6Ω → R_total=10Ω, I=12/10=1.2A.

questions.question: Explain with calculations why adding resistors in parallel increases total current.
questions.answer: Parallel reduces total resistance: 1/R_total=1/R1+1/R2. Lower R_total → higher I_total = V/R_total. Example: R1=6Ω, R2=3Ω, V=12V, 1/R_total=1/6+1/3=0.5 → R_total=2Ω, I_total=12/2=6A.

questions.question: Explain why short circuits can cause fires with numerical reasoning.
questions.answer: Short circuit → very low resistance. Current I = V/R becomes very high, heating wires H = I²R. Example: V=220V, R=0.1Ω → I=220/0.1=2200A, generating extreme heat.

questions.question: Calculate the voltage drop across two series resistors R1=4Ω, R2=6Ω connected to a 12 V battery.
Calculate current through each branch of a parallel circuit with R1=6Ω, R2=12Ω, R3=4Ω, V=12V.
Calculate power dissipated in a resistor of 10Ω with 2A current.
questions.answer: Power P=I²R=2²×10=4×10=40W.

questions.question: Explain why connecting high-wattage appliances without proper fuse rating is hazardous using calculations.
questions.answer: High wattage → higher current. Example: 2200W appliance, V=220V → I=2200/220=10A. Using 5A fuse → fuse blows; without fuse, wire may overheat causing fire.

questions.question: Calculate total energy consumed by three bulbs of 60 W, 40 W, 100 W in parallel for 5 hours at 220 V supply.
questions.answer: Energy=Power×Time. E_total=(60+40+100)×5=200×5=1000Wh=1kWh.

questions.question: Explain using calculations why high-resistance wires produce more heat for the same current.
questions.answer: H=I²R. I constant, higher R → higher H. Example: I=2A, R1=5Ω → H=20W, R2=10Ω → H=40W.

questions.question: Draw and explain a complex series-parallel circuit with numerical calculations for total resistance and current.
questions.answer: Combine series and parallel resistors. Example: R1=4Ω series with R2=6Ω//R3=12Ω. R_parallel=1/(1/6+1/12)=4Ω. R_total=R1+R_parallel=4+4=8Ω. I=V/R_total=12/8=1.5A.

questions.question: Explain using calculations how voltage and current are affected in a series circuit when one resistor is doubled.
questions.answer: Original R_total=R1+R2, I=V/R_total. Doubling R1 → new R_total, I decreases, voltage drop across each R changes: V1=IR1_new, V2=IR2.

questions.question: Explain using calculations how current in parallel branches changes when one branch resistance is halved.
questions.answer: Current in each branch: I=V/R. Halving one branch resistance doubles its current. Total current increases. Example: V=12V, R1=6Ω→I1=2A, R2=12Ω→I2=1A. Halve R1: I1_new=12/3=4A, I_total=4+1+…

questions.question: Explain with diagram and calculation how a fuse protects a series circuit connected to a 12 V battery with a 10 Ω resistor.
questions.answer: Fuse rated 1A: I=V/R=12/10=1.2A > 1A → fuse blows, protecting resistor from excess current.

questions.question: Explain with calculations why AC is used for power transmission and DC is used in electronic devices.
questions.answer: AC can be stepped up using transformers → low loss in transmission. DC provides constant voltage for devices. Example: Power transmitted 220V AC, small device needs 12V DC.

questions.question: Explain the effect of connecting resistors in series and parallel on total power consumed, with calculations.
questions.answer: Series: P=V²/R_total → more R → less power. Parallel: R_total decreases → more total power. Example: Series R_total=10Ω, V=12V → P=144/10=14.4W. Parallel R_total=5Ω → P=144/5=28.8W.

questions.question: Explain with calculations how an electric motor converts electrical energy to mechanical energy.
questions.answer: Electrical energy: Power = VI. Magnetic field generates torque on rotor → rotational motion. Example: V=12V, I=2A, Power=24W, used to rotate motor shaft.

questions.question: Calculate the total current in a combination circuit with R1=4Ω series with (R2=6Ω parallel R3=12Ω) connected to 12 V battery.
questions.answer: R_parallel=1/(1/6+1/12)=4Ω, R_total=R1+R_parallel=4+4=8Ω, I=12/8=1.5A.

questions.question: Explain with calculations how to select proper fuse rating for an appliance of 220 V, 1.5 kW.
questions.answer: I = P/V = 1500/220 ≈ 6.82A. Fuse rating slightly above 6.82A, e.g., 7A, protects appliance safely.
